result.chapter_summary: The author argues that the current campaign against objective testing is a misguided extension of Romantic progressivism that undermines educational excellence and social equity. While acknowledging minor abuses, Hirsch asserts that standardized tests are indispensable for providing incentives, monitoring progress, and ensuring fairness for all students.

claims.text: Mistaken ideas are the root cause of America’s educational problems.
claims.source_passage: Since mistaken ideas have been the root cause of America’s educational problems, the ideas must be changed before the problems can be solved.

claims.text: The educational reforms currently being proposed are themselves part of the problem.
claims.source_passage: the reforms currently being proposed are themselves part of the problem.

claims.text: Effective educational improvement requires practices that progressivists reject, such as factual knowledge, drill and practice, and whole-class instruction.
claims.source_passage: effective improvement must include precisely those practices which are being caricatured and rejected in old-line progressivist attacks on factual knowledge, drill and practice, memorization, whole-class instruction, and other badly needed traditional practices.

claims.text: The repudiation of objective tests is an integral part of a Romantic progressivism dating back to the 1920s.
claims.source_passage: The campaign against giving students tests is an integral part of a Romantic progressivism that goes back to the 1920s.

claims.text: The larger, frequently repeated criticisms of objective tests are not valid.
claims.source_passage: My main task will be to show that the larger, oft-repeated criticisms of objective tests are not valid.

claims.text: Objective tests are necessary in the American context to achieve excellence and fairness.
claims.source_passage: In addition, I shall argue that in the American context such tests are necessary to achieve excellence and fairness.

claims.text: Objective tests function as achievement incentives for students and teachers.
claims.source_passage: They function as achievement incentives for students and teachers, as ways of monitoring students’ progress in order to remedy their deficiencies, and as essential helps in the administrative monitoring of classrooms, schools, and districts.

claims.text: Effective teaching and educational administration are impossible without effective monitoring through testing.
claims.source_passage: Without effective monitoring, neither good teaching nor good educational administration is possible.

claims.text: Objective tests are needed for academic fairness and social equity.
claims.source_passage: Finally, and above all, objective tests are needed for academic fairness and social equity—the chief reasons that Americans, to their credit, have been pioneers in developing objective tests.

claims.text: Properly used tests have an irreplaceably positive effect on learning.
claims.source_passage: Properly used, tests have an irreplaceably positive effect on learning.

claims.text: Learning requires effort.
claims.source_passage: Learning requires effort.

claims.text: High-consequence tests act as spurs to student effort.
claims.source_passage: Tests that carry high consequences have been shown over and over again to act as spurs to effort—a fact that (to the distress of all Romantics) tends to support the Augustinian view of human nature.

claims.text: Authentic assessments are claimed by proponents to be worth teaching to and capable of measuring higher-order skills while defeating negative test-preparation effects.
claims.source_passage: Authentic assessments, it is asserted, are worth teaching to; defeat negative test preparation effects associated with multiple choice tests; give teachers clear models of acceptable outcomes; have a positive influence on instruction and learning; measure higher order skills; and lay bare examinees’ thinking processes.

claims.text: Proponents of national examinations argue that such systems will restore national productivity and global competitiveness.
claims.source_passage: Proponents of a national examination system argue that such a system will motivate unmotivated students; all students will meet world class standards; our nation’s productivity will be increased, and our global competitiveness restored

claims.text: There is an inadequate evidentiary basis for the claims that performance-based assessments yield accuracy, fairness, and educational improvement.
claims.source_passage: Before our policymakers give way to the chorus of endlessly repeated but inadequately founded assertions in favor of performance-based assessments, the proponents should be required to show that exclusive reliance on performance tests does in fact yield accuracy, fairness, and educational improvement.

claims.text: The primary cause of the academic decline in the 1970s was that schools no longer taught or required demanding academic work.
claims.source_passage: Christopher Jencks published a shrewd analysis of the reasons for the academic decline—principally, he thought, because schools no longer taught and required demanding academic work.

claims.text: Standardized tests effectively measure the ability to analyze, synthesize, and draw generalizations.
claims.source_passage: Furthermore, such tests do not measure students’ ability to “analyze, synthesize, draw generalizations, and make applications to new phenomena”—a charge Jencks disposed of by simply quoting sample questions that obviously required those very abilities.

claims.text: The decline in standardized test scores in culturally homogeneous areas like Iowa refutes the claim that the tests are biased toward white middle-class culture.
claims.source_passage: Jencks also refuted the NEA claim that standardized tests were “biased in favor of white middle-class culture” by pointing out that scores had declined sharply in Iowa, which was 98 percent white, and largely “middle class” in values and outlook.

claims.text: The decline in student performance is linked to a contraction in students' total vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: Rather Iowa students’ total vocabulary has almost certainly contracted. It is hard to see how that can be a good thing either in Iowa or elsewhere.

claims.text: The objection that standardized tests contain complex language is an admission that schools are failing to teach students how to read complex prose.
claims.source_passage: If the NEA no longer thinks it is worthwhile to teach students how to read [complex] prose, this may explain why students are less capable of doing so.

claims.text: The term 'standardized test' should be defined specifically as any test that yields the same score for the same performance regardless of the scorer.
claims.source_passage: I shall use the term “standardized test” to mean any objective test, or combination of objective and performance tests, that yields the same score for the same performance, no matter who is doing the scoring.

claims.text: Current attacks on standardized tests are a scapegoating attempt to ignore the ineffectiveness of American schooling methods.
claims.source_passage: I view the war on standardized tests as mainly a disheartened, scapegoating attempt to shoot the messenger that is bringing the bad news.

claims.text: Progressive educational doctrine has been consistently opposed to testing and grading since the 1920s.
claims.source_passage: Orthodox educational doctrine since the 1920s has been consistently opposed to testing and grading.

claims.text: The abolition of tests and grades in progressive schools is a logical extension of discarding a subject-matter curriculum.
claims.source_passage: When William Heard Kilpatrick designed his demonstration school at Teachers College, one of his first innovations, after having dispensed with the subject-matter curriculum, was to abolish tests and grades.

claims.text: Attacks on standardized tests by educators would subside if test scores were higher or more equitably distributed.
claims.source_passage: If our children’s scores on standard tests were getting significantly higher, or if the spread of scores were more equitably distributed by race, class, and gender, or if American kids were further from the bottom on international rankings, these unceasing attacks on standardized tests would subside.

claims.text: Giving number or letter grades to students is considered a fundamental educational mistake in the progressive-Romantic view.
claims.source_passage: In the progressive-Romantic view of education, to give number or letter grades to students in the classroom or on tests is a fundamental educational mistake.

claims.text: Grading sends an implicit message of hierarchy and fosters competition rather than cooperation.
claims.source_passage: It sends an implicit message that one child is better or abler than another, and thus fosters undesirable competition instead of cooperation.

claims.text: The use of standardized measures like grades imposes external rewards and punishments rather than encouraging inward motivation.
claims.source_passage: Kilpatrick and his intellectual descendents argue that the use of such measures imposes external rewards and punishments for learning rather than encouraging an inward motivation toward learning for its own sake.

claims.text: Psychological research contravenes the progressive claim that learning under external incentives is superficial or short-lived.
claims.source_passage: According to this orthodoxy, which is contravened by psychological research, what is learned under compulsion or through external incentives is superficial, artificial, and short-lived.

claims.text: The racial-social objection to testing argues that tests discriminate unfairly based on the proof that certain groups do not receive marks as high as white males.
claims.source_passage: Tests and grades discriminate unfairly against minorities and poor people, and sometimes against females—the proof being that on some measures these groups do not receive average marks as high as those of white males.

claims.text: Resistance to anti-testing criticisms comes primarily from parents and the public rather than the educational community.
claims.source_passage: Resistance to these antitest criticisms comes from parents and the general public rather than from the educational community.

claims.text: Modern terms like 'narrative report cards' and 'portfolios' are merely contemporary dressings for long-standing progressive orthodoxy.
claims.source_passage: Both the desk unbolting and the grading controversies are symbols of the undying influence of progressive orthodoxy, though now dressed up in such modern terminology as “narrative report cards” and “portfolios”

claims.text: Tests and grades strongly contribute to effective teaching.
claims.source_passage: It has been shown convincingly that tests and grades strongly contribute to effective teaching.

claims.text: Students who take courses for a grade study harder and learn more than students who take courses for intrinsic interest alone.
claims.source_passage: Quite unambiguous analysis showed that students who took courses for a grade studied harder and learned more than students who took the courses for intrinsic interest alone.

claims.text: The scientific evidence regarding pass-fail grading systems directly contradicts the claim that giving marks inhibits learning.
claims.source_passage: This scientific confirmation of the common sense of Cranston, Rhode Island, parents runs counter to the claim that “research has shown” that giving marks inhibits learning.

claims.text: The opposition to grades is rooted in Romantic egalitarianism, which seeks to avoid any system where students are labeled as losers.
claims.source_passage: The antigrade view continues to be associated with its origins in Romantic egalitarianism, which declines to accept any version of the idea that “most students will be losers” (i.e., get less than super grades).

claims.text: Jeffersonian democratic egalitarianism is distinct from Romantic egalitarianism because it combines equal opportunity with rigorous meritocratic selection.
claims.source_passage: But this absolute, Romantic version of egalitarianism is very different from Jeffersonian democratic egalitarianism, which aimed to give rich and poor the same foundations for achievement, but to be quite rigorous in selecting only the better students for subsequent free education through a system of tests and grades.

claims.text: Educational reformers are attempting to 're-educate' the public to favor equal results (substantive equity) over meritocratic opportunity (procedural equity).
claims.source_passage: the test expert recommended “re-educating” the public to favor equal results for everyone by appealing to their “self interest for a better society.”

claims.text: The push for nonstandardized tests is a strategy to ensure that all demographic groups appear to perform the same.
claims.source_passage: This improvement is to be accomplished by repudiating standardized tests in favor of more “equitable” nonstandardized kinds which will ensure that every group performs the same.

claims.text: Performance-based assessment is not feasible for large-scale K-12 testing because it cannot be accurate, fair, and cost-effective simultaneously.
claims.source_passage: But my own research in performance-based assessment, coupled with the researches of others, caused me after a time to give up hope (for reasons I shall present) that such an approach to large-scale assessment could possibly be accurate, fair, and reasonable in cost.

claims.text: Standardized tests have considerable strengths and are superior to performance tests for summative evaluation when properly used.
claims.source_passage: I should like to acquaint the reader with the other side of the research story, which exposes some of the defects of performance tests and some of the very considerable strengths of standardized ones—when properly used.

claims.text: Teacher-graded student work is highly subjective and inconsistent, often resulting in different grades for the same quality of work.
claims.source_passage: Students have long believed (on good evidence) that if the same paper is submitted to two teachers in two different sections of the same course, the paper is likely to receive two very different grades.

claims.text: Experienced teachers grading essays rarely achieve a correlation greater than .40 in their grading.
claims.source_passage: Even when the raters were experienced teachers, the grades given to the papers by the different raters never attained a correlation greater than .40.

claims.text: Even experienced teachers demonstrate extremely low correlation in the grades they assign to the same student essays.
claims.source_passage: Even when the raters were experienced teachers, the grades given to the papers by the different raters never attained a correlation greater than .40.

claims.text: The agreement achieved between graders in professional performance assessment sessions is arbitrary and unstable.
claims.source_passage: But the ETS has also discovered this momentarily achieved agreement to be arbitrary and unstable. Although the various readers in a small group could be socialized to share similar explicit and implicit grading principles, the grades of their group would differ from those of a different group at the same session.

claims.text: Performance-based test scores often depend more on the specific grader or the year of the exam than on the quality of the student's work.
claims.source_passage: the high-stakes grades given to a student’s performance-based test ‘might well depend more on which year he appeared for the examination, or on which person read his paper, than it would on what he had written.’

claims.text: An inexpungible arbitrariness lies at the heart of grading performance-based assessments.
claims.source_passage: But even when fairness is achieved, an inexpungible arbitrariness lies at the heart of grading performance-based assessments.

claims.text: Graders disagree fundamentally on the relative weights that should be awarded to different elements of a performance, such as ideas versus organization.
claims.source_passage: even when they agree on the elements that make for high quality, they may disagree about the relative weights that should be awarded to those elements. Should quality of ideas, for instance, count for more in writing than quality of organization?

claims.text: Individual performance varies significantly based on the specific task (task variability) rather than just general ability.
claims.source_passage: The more important performer inconsistency lies in task variability: some pianists play Liszt a lot better than they do Mozart; some kids write better about snakes than they do about computers.

claims.text: There is no such thing as generalized, homogeneous reading or writing ability.
claims.source_passage: There is just no such thing as equal, across-the-board writing ability any more than there is generalized, homogeneous reading ability.

claims.text: Performance quality is primarily determined by a student's level of familiarity with the specific task or subject matter.
claims.source_passage: A person’s performance varies not just because of temperamental congeniality with a certain task but, mainly, because of his or her level of familiarity with the task. People tend to craft technically superior papers about subjects they happen to be familiar with.

claims.text: Cognitive load constraints in working memory explain why performance declines when a student is unfamiliar with a topic.
claims.source_passage: When a task is unfamiliar and requires a great deal of attention in dealing with its middle-level components, the limited capacity of working memory leaves little room for monitoring the various lower- or higher-level components of the task.

claims.text: A single writing sample is an inaccurate and misleading measure of a student's average ability.
claims.source_passage: One writing sample, even when graded by socialized graders, may yield a very misleading inference about a student’s average writing ability.

claims.text: The unreliability of scoring in large-scale performance-based systems (like Vermont's) renders them useless for most intended educational purposes.
claims.source_passage: The unreliability of scoring alone was sufficient to preclude most of the intended uses of the scores.

claims.text: A single writing sample may yield a misleading inference about a student’s average writing ability due to topic familiarity.
claims.source_passage: If the topic is especially familiar and congenial to a particular student, the performance is likely to be uncharacteristically good. Conversely, if the topic is unfamiliar, it is likely to be uncharacteristically bad.

claims.text: Performance-based assessments graded by raters have a low likelihood of accurately assessing a student’s average ability to perform.
claims.source_passage: Given these inconsistencies of both the grader and the performer, it is no surprise that a performance-based assessment, graded by a single trained rater or even by a group of trained raters, has a low likelihood of accurately assessing a student’s average ability to perform.

claims.text: Portfolio-based rating systems fail to discern real differences in student work quality.
claims.source_passage: The rating system failed to discern real differences in quality.

claims.text: The financial costs of large-scale performance-based testing are sizeable.
claims.source_passage: The financial costs appear to be sizeable.

claims.text: Performance-based assessments require multiple topics and dozens of independent readings to reach a reasonably sound conclusion about a student's ability.
claims.source_passage: Thus, by training the graders and by having five set topics and twenty-five independent readings for each student, it was possible to reach a reasonably sound conclusion about each student’s average writing ability.

claims.text: Assigning three shorter papers is fairer to the student than assigning a single longer paper.
claims.source_passage: We know from other research that assigning three twenty-minute topics is fairer to the student than assigning a single one-hour paper.

claims.text: Multiple-choice sections achieve higher accuracy and fairness at a lower cost than multiple essay readings.
claims.source_passage: In short, much higher accuracy and fairness (.775 vs. .640) are obtained by using three multiple-choice sections than by using three essays that are given three readings each. Moreover, the better result is obtained at a fraction of the cost of reading three papers three times.

claims.text: A combination of multiple-choice items plus one essay is the best and fairest test of writing ability.
claims.source_passage: A combination of multiple-choice items plus one essay was the best and fairest test of all.

claims.text: The primary advantage of including an essay in a standardized test is the pedagogical model it provides rather than its statistical validity.
claims.source_passage: Rather, the advantage has to be assessed in terms of the model the essay provides for students and teachers. An essay in the English Composition Test says to the student that skill in actual writing is an important outcome of instruction.

claims.text: A test consisting of multiple-choice segments combined with a writing sample yields higher validity than an objective test alone.
claims.source_passage: nd his colleagues determined that an hour test consisting of two multiple-choice segments plus a writing sample yielded a still higher test validity against the criterion—that is, .784 vs. .775.

claims.text: The slight increase in validity gained by adding an essay to an objective test does not, on its own, justify the increased financial cost.
claims.source_passage: It is doubtful that the slight increase in validity alone can justify the increased cost.

claims.text: An essay requirement signals to students that actual writing skill is a vital outcome of instruction and to teachers that multiple-choice success is insufficient evidence of effective teaching.
claims.source_passage: An essay in the English Composition Test says to the student that skill in actual writing is an important outcome of instruction. It says to the teacher that the ability to answer multiple-choice questions, unless accompanied by the ability to compose answers to essay questions, is not sufficient evidence of effective teaching.

claims.text: Effective testing policy should balance performance-based and multiple-choice tests rather than relinquishing either.
claims.source_passage: By including a performance component, policymakers can ensure that these tests are sending out the right educational messages to students and teachers; but at the same time, by including a generous multiple-choice component, they can ensure accuracy and fairness at a reasonable cost.

claims.text: Fair and accurate performance assessments cannot be achieved on a large scale at a reasonable cost.
claims.source_passage: Research has repeatedly shown that fair and accurate performance assessments cannot be achieved on a large scale at a reasonable cost.

claims.text: The ongoing attack on multiple-choice tests is a smear campaign designed to discredit all reliable means of accountability for students and schools.
claims.source_passage: We will be left with what some people apparently want—a successful discrediting of all reliable means of accountability for students and schools.

claims.text: Most complaints against standardized tests are unjustified once the specific abuses of those tests are corrected.
claims.source_passage: But once the abuses of standardized tests are corrected, the remaining complaints against them can be shown to be unjustified and misguided.

claims.text: A test is primarily a sampling device, and its fairness depends on the size and representativeness of the sample taken from the domain.
claims.source_passage: A test is primarily a sampling device. The principal reason that a good objective test is fairer than a good performance test is that, for a test of any reasonable length, a good objective test can probe a much larger and more representative sample of the domain being sampled.

claims.text: Objective tests sample a larger and more representative variety of factors within a skill or field of knowledge than performance tests of the same length.
claims.source_passage: A performance test of writing, for instance, adequately covers just one or two genres of writing, whereas an objective test can sample five or six.

claims.text: An objective test of eighty items typically achieves high reliability, with additional items yielding diminishing returns.
claims.source_passage: Typically, an objective test of eighty items for a particular domain will have a very high reliability. . . After that point, the law of diminishing returns sets in dramatically.

claims.text: A multiple-choice test is a more valid and reliable measure of writing ability than a performance-based test of equal length.
claims.source_passage: In the Godshalk study, discussed above, it was demonstrated that a multiple-choice test is a far more valid and reliable measure of writing ability than a performance-based test of the same length.

claims.text: For high-stakes summative tests, objective tests are superior to all other available systems despite their surface-level flaws.
claims.source_passage: The reason the psychometric community defends their use for high-stakes, summative tests is that, bad as objective tests might seem to be, they are, as Churchill said of democracy, better than all the other systems.

claims.text: Antitesters exploit repellent features of objective tests, such as student fatigue and the perception that they don't measure higher-order skills.
claims.source_passage: On the surface, there are several aspects of these items that seem designed to repel a sensitive observer. Antitesters have been quick to exploit these repellent features. First of all, young people are very tired of taking tests like these.

claims.text: Objective tests were not designed for classroom use by individual teachers.
claims.source_passage: As a teacher, I would find such objections alone decisive, and in fact I do not use such tests in my classroom, nor were the tests designed for such use.

claims.text: Early test proponents relied on consistent correlations between test scores and real-world criteria rather than minute analysis of how items work.
claims.source_passage: The early proponents of objective tests did not pause to analyze in minute detail how they work in particular cases. It was enough to show that there was a strong and consistent correlation between the criterion (in this case, the criterion of actual writing ability) and the test.

claims.text: Modern test theory requires explaining objective correlations with a coherent theory of why those correlations exist.
claims.source_passage: Test makers are being asked to explain and justify the objective correlations between tests and the real world with an adequate and coherent theory of why the correlations exist.

claims.text: The distinction between editing ability and writing ability is highly oversimplified, especially under test conditions.
claims.source_passage: The distinction between editing ability and writing ability is highly oversimplified—especially under test conditions.

claims.text: The creative and constructive side of writing is not well sampled even in the best performance tests because of time and topic constraints.
claims.source_passage: The creative, constructive side of writing is not well sampled even in the best performance tests, since no reliable performance test can offer free choice of topics and unlimited time.

claims.text: Excellence in editing and excellence in writing are inextricable, as best writers edit mentally before writing.
claims.source_passage: The quality of real-world writing is much more dependent on editing ability than on facility in initially getting words down on paper. The best writers edit in their minds before they write. Excellence in editing and excellence in writing are inextricable.

claims.text: Objective English questions do not focus exclusively on superficial aspects of writing ability.
claims.source_passage: Before concluding that objective English questions focus on only superficial aspects of writing ability, one ought to give serious consideration to the data reported here.

claims.text: The belief that multiple-choice tests inherently impose superficiality and rote memorization is a form of stereotyping that ignores their substance.
claims.source_passage: The more general idea that the form of multiple-choice tests imposes superficiality, rote memorization, and fragmentation upon students and teachers is an exemplary case of judging a book by its cover.

claims.text: A sequence of objective questions can gauge the depth and breadth of knowledge through ingenuity and wit.
claims.source_passage: A sonnet sequence can accomplish epic ambitions, and a sequence of objective questions can swiftly gauge the depth and breadth of a person’s knowledge, sometimes by using a great deal of ingenuity and wit.

claims.text: Teaching narrowly to high-stakes, continually reused tests corrupts education.
claims.source_passage: First, the practice of teaching narrowly to high-stakes, continually reused tests, instead of teaching to the subject, often corrupts education in the schools.

claims.text: Using off-the-shelf tests as primary tools of reform enables policymakers to evade difficult decisions regarding educational goals.
claims.source_passage: it is an abuse of these off-the-shelf tests to rely on them as primary tools of educational reform—a convenient practice that enables policymakers to evade hard decisions about educational goals and strategies.

claims.text: Legislators and the public use test scores as a blunt and ineffective instrument of compulsion because they lack knowledge of what is being tested.
claims.source_passage: Legislators and the general public, lacking firsthand knowledge of what is being tested, have tended to use test scores as a blunt (and ineffective) instrument of compulsion, and have neglected the intellectually and politically difficult task of setting forth precise goals.

claims.text: Deciding educational and social goals is the duty of society as a whole, not test makers.
claims.source_passage: The duty to decide on such goals properly belongs to society as a whole—to parents, teachers, and the representatives of the people—not to test makers.

claims.text: Teaching to the test instead of teaching to the subject often corrupts education in schools.
claims.source_passage: Teaching to the test, instead of teaching to the subject, often corrupts education in the schools.

claims.text: Society—including parents, teachers, and representatives—has the duty to decide educational goals, not test makers.
claims.source_passage: The duty to decide on such goals properly belongs to society as a whole—to parents, teachers, and the representatives of the people—not to test makers.

claims.text: Multiple-choice tests are particularly vulnerable to instruction fragmentation because they probe specific points in specific ways.
claims.source_passage: Multiple-choice tests are particularly vulnerable to this sort of abuse because they probe knowledge and skill at very specific points and in very specific ways.

claims.text: Fragmented, test-specific instruction allows unskilled students and incompetent administrators to appear more capable than they are.
claims.source_passage: Such fragmented and irresponsible teaching is practiced, and it does enable unskilled students to appear more skilled (and teachers and administrators more competent) than they really are.

claims.text: Pedagogical abuses of tests are used as a pretext by some educators to avoid meaningful accountability altogether.
claims.source_passage: The pedagogical abuses of objective tests are prevalent enough to give surface respectability to those who exploit such test abuses as a pretext for avoiding meaningful accountability altogether.

claims.text: Matrix testing provides a 'no-fault diagnosis' that lacks individual incentives and classroom-level monitoring.
claims.source_passage: it provides few incentives or disincentives to individuals, little monitoring, and no real accountability at the classroom level.

claims.text: California's decline to the bottom of national reading scores resulted from combining nonaccountable matrix testing with naturalistic reading instruction.
claims.source_passage: when such individually nonaccountable testing has been coupled with a state policy that favors naturalistic teaching of reading, the result was California’s dropping to the bottom in national reading scores.

claims.text: Standardized testing is an absolute necessity for holding individual teachers and students accountable for reading progress.
claims.source_passage: The teaching of reading offers a good example of the absolute need for standardized testing as a tool of accountability for individual teachers and students.

claims.text: Every child who is not organically impaired can be brought to reading competence by the end of first grade.
claims.source_passage: I am told by researchers in the area of beginning reading that every child who is not organically impaired can be brought to an appropriate degree of reading competence by the end of first grade.

claims.text: Modern educational systems have a duty to provide ongoing diagnostic tests and a standardized reading test for every child at the end of first grade.
claims.source_passage: Ongoing diagnostic tests and a standardized reading test given to each child at the end of first grade are absolute duties of every modern educational system.

claims.text: Providing tests without guidance or guidance without tests constitutes an abandonment of adult responsibility to children.
claims.source_passage: Testing without guidance is as irresponsible as guidance without testing. Neither side of the equation should be neglected.

claims.text: 'Authentic' types of reading tests are uncontrolled, unfair, and unable to replicate real-world reading performance.
claims.source_passage: What this argument fails to disclose, however, is that more “authentic” types of reading tests are not only uncontrolled, hence unfair, but also unable to replicate real-world reading.

claims.text: Even the lowest-quality standardized reading tests correlate well with high-quality ones and real-world reading abilities.
claims.source_passage: But at the present time, even the worst standardized reading tests, with all their faults, correlate well with the best ones—and with real-world reading abilities.

claims.text: Testing students without providing educational guidance is as irresponsible as providing guidance without testing.
claims.source_passage: Testing without guidance is as irresponsible as guidance without testing. Neither side of the equation should be neglected.

claims.text: No school-based performance can truly reproduce performance in the real world.
claims.source_passage: No “performance” that takes place in school reproduces performance in the real world.

claims.text: The educational misuse of tests, such as prepping students for specific known samples, constitutes a form of cheating.
claims.source_passage: The educational misuse of these tests consists mainly, and in varying degrees, of what can only properly be termed “cheating”—sometimes outright, sometimes subtle.

claims.text: Specific test preparation for known items prioritizes adult convenience over the long-term well-being of children.
claims.source_passage: To know the sample in advance and to prep students for it specifically is a prime example of putting the temporary convenience of adults above the long-range good of children.

claims.text: Familiarity with specific test items leads to scores that significantly overstate a student's actual knowledge and skill.
claims.source_passage: This result meant that the district’s usual test scores were significantly overstating students’ knowledge and skill—a distortion that is undoubtedly widespread.

claims.text: The root cause of score inflation is teachers focusing instruction narrowly on the specific items they know will be tested.
claims.source_passage: Clearly, the most probable reason for the difference in student performance on the two tests was that teachers were focusing their instruction narrowly on the specific kinds of items that would be tested—precisely in order to help achieve the misleadingly favorable result.

claims.text: Standardized tests are the most effective instruments available for demonstrating and measuring the corrupting influence of test misuse.
claims.source_passage: Notice, however, that standardized tests were the very instruments used in the experiment to demonstrate this baleful influence. It is hard to imagine what other tool could have been so effectively employed for the purpose

claims.text: Unstandardized tests are inherently unfair.
claims.source_passage: unstandardized tests, after all, are inherently unfair

claims.text: Test misuse can be easily prevented by delaying the selection of the specific test form until the last possible minute.
claims.source_passage: Can such misuse be prevented? Yes, easily, and the Koretz experiment indicates the simplest preventive approach. A district should not determine which form of a test will be used until the last possible minute.

claims.text: If students are required to be prepared for a large number of parallel test forms, teaching to the test becomes equivalent to teaching the entire subject domain.
claims.source_passage: It would be difficult if not impossible to outsmart such a system, since to teach the right answers to all the many forms of the test would be more or less equivalent to teaching the whole domain.

claims.text: There exists a tacit collusion between administrators and teachers to keep test scores inaccurately high.
claims.source_passage: That there exists instead a lot of tacit collusion aimed at keeping test scores inaccurately high, and a lot of silent pressure on teachers to teach narrowly in order to do so, is not widely known.

claims.text: The press frequently focuses on reporting scandals rather than the more subtle corruption of the educational process itself.
claims.source_passage: The press ignored the more subtle corruption of testing and of the entire educational process. Lack of public awareness of this more subtle test misuse is another illustration of the need for savvy and independent probing by the reporters who cover education.

claims.text: There exists a tacit collusion in the educational system aimed at keeping test scores inaccurately high.
claims.source_passage: That there exists instead a lot of tacit collusion aimed at keeping test scores inaccurately high, and a lot of silent pressure on teachers to teach narrowly in order to do so, is not widely known.

claims.text: The press has ignored the subtle corruption of the testing process, focusing instead on reporting scandals like the Lake Wobegon effect.
claims.source_passage: The press ignored the more subtle corruption of testing and of the entire educational process. Lack of public awareness of this more subtle test misuse is another illustration of the need for savvy and independent probing by the reporters who cover education.

claims.text: The use of multiple parallel test forms is an effective solution to the problem of teaching narrowly to a specific test.
claims.source_passage: Should that happen, the chief abuse of standardized tests—the one really convincing educational objection to using them (i.e., teaching narrowly to the specific test)—will have been removed.

claims.text: The use of multiple parallel test forms is financially feasible for any school district because the main cost is grading, not printing.
claims.source_passage: The costs of this change in testing practice are not prohibitive. The main expense of standardized testing lies not in printing the test booklets but in grading the tests. Hence, the use of multiple parallel test forms is a solution within the financial reach of any district.

claims.text: The only truly convincing educational objection to standardized tests is the practice of teaching narrowly to the specific test content.
claims.source_passage: Should that happen, the chief abuse of standardized tests—the one really convincing educational objection to using them (i.e., teaching narrowly to the specific test)—will have been removed.

claims.text: Some existing multiple-choice tests encourage superficiality and passivity, but others do not.
claims.source_passage: It is true that some of the existing multiple-choice tests do encourage superficiality and passivity. But it is also true that some do not.

claims.text: Proposed performance tests are currently neither reliable nor fair as alternatives to standardized tests.
claims.source_passage: And since the proposed alternatives (i.e., performance tests) are not by themselves reliable or fair, a successful campaign to abolish standardized tests would result in our schools becoming even less equitable and our students less competent than they now are.

claims.text: Abolishing standardized tests would lead to less equity in schools and lower student competence.
claims.source_passage: a successful campaign to abolish standardized tests would result in our schools becoming even less equitable and our students less competent than they now are.

claims.text: The antitest movement relies on half-truths that must be examined to prevent them from being believed.
claims.source_passage: the exercise is important to undertake on the chance that it will help make the half-truths of the antitest movement less likely to be believed.

claims.text: Current research suggests many standardized tests fail to measure higher-order cognitive abilities or real-world tasks.
claims.source_passage: Current research on human learning and performance has suggested that many currently used tests fail to measure students higher order cognitive abilities or to support their capacities to perform real world tasks.

claims.text: If students are consistently given tests that do not require or measure higher-order skills, then those tests will not encourage the teaching of higher-order skills.
claims.source_passage: It is certainly true that if students are consistently given tests that do not require or measure higher-order skills, then those tests will not encourage the teaching of higher-order skills.

claims.text: A superficial and undemanding test is superficial and undemanding regardless of whether it is in multiple-choice or essay format.
claims.source_passage: A superficial and undemanding test is superficial and undemanding whether in multiple-choice or in essay form.

claims.text: Critics of standardized testing have failed to demonstrate that the multiple-choice format itself causes tests to probe only lower-order skills.
claims.source_passage: But, so far, complaints like the ones quoted above have not identified any particular inferior tests or demonstrated that their being put in multiple-choice format causes them to probe only lower-order skills.

claims.text: The multiple-choice format does not inherently impose passivity or fragmentation of thought on the test-taker.
claims.source_passage: it took just a few minutes to create the following question, which, for all its faults, easily refutes the claim that the multiple-choice format imposes passivity and fragmentation of thought

claims.text: The multiple-choice format cannot test creativity in solving real-world problems because the format does not allow for the production of new ideas or hypotheses.
claims.source_passage: What the multiple-choice format cannot test, Frederiksen showed, is creativity in solving real-world problems. That is because the form of the test does not allow for the production of new ideas or hypotheses, since each of the choices has already been formed.

claims.text: There is a widespread belief, though contested, that creativity cannot be taught.
claims.source_passage: It is certainly desirable to encourage creativity, but many believe it cannot be taught.

claims.text: Fair and accurate performance tests of productive skills have consistently failed to materialize even in high-stakes fields like medicine.
claims.source_passage: That these conceptual and practical problems have not been solved, and are not likely to be, was the theme of a recent review article by Swanson and others, which showed that in the area of study where real-world higher-order skills have been most consistently tested—medicine—the decades-long attempt to produce fair and accurate performance tests of productive skills has ended in failure.

claims.text: Expertise is not a general trait; performance in one context does not predict performance in other contexts.
claims.source_passage: The article is particularly useful in setting forth the reasons for this failure: that scoring is highly inconsistent; that “generalizability is low”; that “expertise is not a general trait”; that simulations are not the real world, and “examinees do not behave in the same way they would in real life”; “that performance in one context does not predict performance in other contexts”

claims.text: The pursuit of performance-based assessment has ironically led to the improvement of multiple-choice tests.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, Swanson and his colleagues concluded that a “consequence of enthusiasm for performance-based assessment in the health professions is, ironically, the improvement of multiple-choice tests.”

claims.text: There is no evidence of 'format factors' in testing; multiple-choice and open formats measure the same attributes.
claims.source_passage: Investigators in general, and sometimes to their surprise, “have found no evidence of format factors.” And “tests of mathematical reasoning measure the same attribute regardless of test format.”

claims.text: Research rejects the claim that the multiple-choice format is condemned to probe only surface-level facts rather than deep understanding.
claims.source_passage: As for the notion that the multiple-choice format is condemned to probe only surfaces rather than deep understanding, Frederiksen was quite explicit in stating that research had rejected the claim

claims.text: The test format (multiple-choice versus other types) does not significantly influence what a test actually measures.
claims.source_passage: The correlations are generally interpreted to mean that the format does not influence what a test measures.

claims.text: Tests of mathematical reasoning measure the same attribute regardless of whether the format is multiple-choice or another type.
claims.source_passage: And 'tests of mathematical reasoning measure the same attribute regardless of test format.'

claims.text: Anti-testing advocates cite research that actually repudiates their own claims regarding the limitations of multiple-choice items.
claims.source_passage: These clear repudiations of anti-standardized-test claims are being stated not by a defender of multiple-choice tests but, rather, in a research report cited by anti-testers as lending scientific support to their position!

claims.text: Multiple-choice questions can be designed to require students to generate ideas and structure tasks.
claims.source_passage: Let’s see if we can generate one or two test items that do require students to generate ideas and structure tasks. The exercise turns out not to be hard.

claims.text: Correctly answering complex multiple-choice questions in real-world scenarios requires relevant background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: They would need to engage in productive thought and to possess—as in all real-world problem-solving situations—relevant background knowledge.

claims.text: Relevant knowledge acts as a shortcut in real-world problem solving regardless of the test format used.
claims.source_passage: But no matter what the test formats, relevant knowledge is always a shortcut in real-world problem solving

claims.text: The accusation that standardized tests are restricted to rote learning is particularly misguided in the domain of mathematics.
claims.source_passage: Nowhere is the accusation of triviality and rote learning more misguided than in the domain of mathematics.

claims.text: A well-constructed standardized test of math or reading is inherently a performance test requiring information integration and idea generation.
claims.source_passage: A well-constructed and designed standardized test of math or reading is inherently a performance test which requires the “integration of information and the generation of ideas” and which “engages [students’] capacities to structure tasks, produce ideas, and solve problems.”

claims.text: Standardized reading tests require active production because students must productively read and comprehend passages to answer questions.
claims.source_passage: After all, students must productively read and comprehend passages before they can answer multiple-choice questions about them

claims.text: It is possible to include a performance component directly in standardized tests by requiring students to write down computations.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, it is possible to include a performance component directly in such tests by requiring, as some math tests do, that the student write down all computations in spaces provided near the question.

claims.text: Standardized reading and math tests have a built-in productive or performance component.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, in two of the most important kinds of tests given in elementary schools, reading tests and math tests, there is a built-in productive or performance component in the tests themselves.

claims.text: Solving complex multiple-choice math problems requires the productive ability to determine which computation is applicable, not just computational skill.
claims.source_passage: Thus, it is not the computation itself that requires productive, problem-solving abilities. Rather, it is figuring out which computation would be applicable. Mere computational skill is not enough.

claims.text: Many educational doctrines become axiomatic through constant repetition rather than through being correct.
claims.source_passage: many educational doctrines have become axiomatic not by being correct but by being repeated so often that it seems they must be correct.

claims.text: The charge that multiple-choice tests encourage passivity is only valid when the tests are used corruptly by teaching to specific, repeated items.
claims.source_passage: But its validity can only come from the corrupt use of these tests. If a teacher knows that there will be a smog question and an apple-basket question and a perpendicular-line question, then most children in the district will probably be able to answer all the above examples by passive recall and rote memorization.

claims.text: Since 1970, basic test scores have increased slightly while higher-order thinking skills have declined in virtually all subject areas.
claims.source_passage: Since about 1970, when standardized tests began to be used for a wider variety of accountability purposes, basic test scores have been increasing slightly, while assessments of higher order thinking skills have declined in virtually all subject areas.

claims.text: The argument that multiple-choice tests caused the decline in higher-order skills is a 'post hoc, ergo propter hoc' fallacy.
claims.source_passage: The premise of this post hoc, ergo propter hoc thinking is that multiple-choice tests can measure only basic skills. This would explain why they have induced an improvement in such skills, accompanied by a diminishment in the more desirable higher skills that they supposedly cannot measure and do not encourage.

claims.text: The NAEP's ability to measure the decline in higher skills proves that multiple-choice tests are capable of measuring those skills.
claims.source_passage: If the NAEP test items could not measure higher skills, how could they tell us that they had declined?

claims.text: The decline in higher-order skills was caused by state tests focusing on lower skills and providing no incentives for teaching higher ones.
claims.source_passage: But so long as tests remained focused on lower skills, and so long as the states provided no incentives for teaching higher ones, the practical effect of the basic-skills movement was indeed to neglect higher-order skills.

claims.text: Standardized tests can help achieve improvements in higher-order skills in the same way they helped improve lower-order skills.
claims.source_passage: they could in fact help achieve the same sort of improvement in higher skills as they have accomplished in lower ones—an inference that has been eloquently argued by Barbara Lerner.

claims.text: The basic-skills movement neglected higher-order skills because states provided no incentives for teaching them and tests remained focused on lower skills, not because test formats were incapable of measuring higher skills.
claims.source_passage: But so long as tests remained focused on lower skills, and so long as the states provided no incentives for teaching higher ones, the practical effect of the basic-skills movement was indeed to neglect higher-order skills. The mistake in the argument lies in the suggestion that the format of the tests prevents them from testing higher skills

claims.text: Basic skills are the necessary and only possible foundation for higher-order skills.
claims.source_passage: Basic skills are no bar to higher skills but are in fact their only possible foundation.

claims.text: It is unlikely that any significant degree of technical bias exists in modern high-stakes multiple-choice tests.
claims.source_passage: Test makers in recent years have taken pains to ensure the absence of bias in this technical sense, and it is unlikely that any significant degree of technical bias exists in present-day high-stakes multiple-choice tests.

claims.text: Differences in group test performance do not denote technical bias if they reflect actual differences in real-world performance on the construct being measured.
claims.source_passage: If twelve-year-old girls perform worse on math tests, and also perform worse on real-world math problems, then a score differential between the sexes does not in itself reflect technical test bias or unfairness.

claims.text: The issue of test fairness is distinct from the issue of technical test bias and should be addressed separately.
claims.source_passage: The issue of fairness, which is a major theme of this book, needs to be addressed separately from the issue of technical test bias

claims.text: Critiques of technical test bias function primarily to create a confusion between technical measurement issues and ideological disagreements.
claims.source_passage: technical test bias... is a distraction whose chief function is to create a convenient confusion between the technical and the ideological.

claims.text: Mathematical 'renorming' to eliminate group score gaps lifts group means without changing the underlying reality of educational attainment.
claims.source_passage: lifting, with the wave of a formula, the mean of the entire country without changing the underlying reality.

claims.text: Adjusting test scores to achieve equity is a practical injustice to disadvantaged students because high scores without real competency do not lead to social equality.
claims.source_passage: the gravest objection to attempting to achieve educational equity through adjusting test scores is the practical injustice it commits upon those it proposes to help. Getting a high score on a test is not the ticket to social equality for students whose actual educational competencies are low

claims.text: Real social justice depends more on real-world competencies and economic improvement than on school grades or adjusted test scores.
claims.source_passage: Real social justice lies beyond the sheltered halls of school and college—in improving the relative economic condition of groups that have been both economically and educationally depressed. Improvement in one’s economic condition depends nowadays more upon one’s real-world competencies than upon one’s grades or scores

claims.text: The practice of renorming tests selectively to remove score differences between groups should not be accepted.
claims.source_passage: people might not accept and (according to some solid analysis by Linda Gottfredson) should not accept the practice of renorming selectively to remove the score differences between groups.

claims.text: Attempting to achieve educational equity through adjusting test scores commits a practical injustice upon the students it proposes to help.
claims.source_passage: But surely the gravest objection to attempting to achieve educational equity through adjusting test scores is the practical injustice it commits upon those it proposes to help.

claims.text: A high test score is not a ticket to social equality if a student's actual educational competencies are low.
claims.source_passage: Getting a high score on a test is not the ticket to social equality for students whose actual educational competencies are low, no matter what scores or grades have been attached to their performances.

claims.text: A wage earner's nominal educational level is a misleading statistic in an era of social promotion.
claims.source_passage: But a wage earner’s nominal educational level can be a very misleading statistic in an era of social promotion to the next grade for every level of schooling.

claims.text: The wage gap between races closes dramatically when black-white comparisons are made based on actual educational achievement rather than nominal education level.
claims.source_passage: Instead, Ferguson studied black-white wage inequality in terms of actual educational achievement. Once he made that adjustment, he found that the wage gap between the races closed dramatically.

claims.text: Standardized multiple-choice tests, such as the AFQT, accurately probe real-world competencies.
claims.source_passage: Ferguson’s work is thus yet another refutation of the claim that multiple-choice tests do not probe real-world competencies.

claims.text: Standardized tests are not technically biased against any group because they correlate with actual economic achievement and job performance regardless of race.
claims.source_passage: Since scores on these tests correlate highly with actual economic achievement and job performance regardless of race, they are not biased against any group with regard to real-world competency.

claims.text: The charge of 'cultural bias' in testing is often used to support policies that force schools to adjust to home cultures rather than teaching mainstream competencies.
claims.source_passage: As far as I can see, the accusation is leveled in order to support a policy that will oblige an adjustment in what schools teach and tests test, to ensure that the home cultures of various groups are not discriminated against.

claims.text: Most minority parents accept and desire that schools teach mainstream science, mathematics, and language arts.
claims.source_passage: But most parents, including most minority parents, accept the idea that schools should teach mainstream science, mathematics, or language arts.

claims.text: The marketplace functions as a commons that erases ethnic distinctions.
claims.source_passage: The marketplace is a commons. It erases ethnic distinctions.

claims.text: Mainstream hybrid culture is a tool for communication between strangers rather than an essential, identity-defining culture of a specific group.
claims.source_passage: The notion that such a hybrid culture, devised to enable communication between strangers, is somebody’s essential, identity-defining culture is a historical and an economic mistake.

claims.text: Minority families sign up in disproportionate numbers for public schools that offer effective mainstream academic training in reading, writing, and mathematics.
claims.source_passage: Wherever public schools have offered the choice of truly effective mainstream academic training in reading, writing, and mathematics, minority families have signed up in disproportionate numbers, and their children have responded eagerly and well.

claims.text: Minority parents recognize a direct connection between their children's economic advancement and the mastery of mainstream American culture.
claims.source_passage: These parents clearly recognize the direct connection between economic advancement for their children and the mastery of the culture of the marketplace, which in the United States is mainstream culture.

claims.text: Standard written forms of major languages like Koine, Latin, and English are hybrids created by the marketplace to enable communication between strangers.
claims.source_passage: The standard forms of those written languages are hybrids created in and by the marketplace. The notion that such a hybrid culture, devised to enable communication between strangers, is somebody’s essential, identity-defining culture is a historical and an economic mistake.

claims.text: The belief that home language and culture are central to a person's essence is a mistake rooted in Romanticism.
claims.source_passage: It is a mistake that, like many other ideas in our intellectual history, must be laid at the door of Romanticism. . . the Romantics invented the idea that one’s home language and culture were central to one’s very essence.

claims.text: Effective classroom schooling must be monocultural to allow all students to participate in the public marketplace.
claims.source_passage: What this account fails to state is that effective classroom schooling has to be monocultural for the same reason the marketplace has to be—so that all can participate.

claims.text: Modern American school culture is a hybrid rather than a pure 'Eurocentric' or 'Anglocentric' tradition.
claims.source_passage: But it is misleading to suggest that this monocultural school-based culture is not itself a hybrid, as lingua francas always are. In the present-day American school, it is difficult to find any examples of pure-bred “Eurocentric” or “Anglocentric” instruction.

claims.text: Balkanized schooling and testing will fail to lead to social and economic equity.
claims.source_passage: Time, experience, and a profounder sense of fairness will, one hopes, repudiate as impractical the idea that Balkanized schooling or testing could lead to social and economic equity.

claims.text: In the United States, the educational debate is split between defining fairness as 'equality of opportunity' (favored by conservatives) versus 'equality of result' (favored by progressives).
claims.source_passage: In many democracies of the world, and especially in the United States, the debate has focused on whether fairness should be defined as “equality of opportunity” or “equality of result,” with both sides pronouncing themselves in favor of “true fairness.”

claims.text: Meritocrats believe in tracking to separate talented students from less talented ones so each can reach their potential without being held back.
claims.source_passage: On one side are those who might be called meritocrats or talent-trackers, who believe that fairness consists in identifying and separating talented students from less talented ones so that every child can attain his or her potential without being held back.

claims.text: Egalitarians oppose tracking because it discourages disadvantaged students and deprives abler ones of democratic leadership training.
claims.source_passage: On the other side are egalitarians or inclusivists who, in opposition to all tracking, especially in early grades, hold that such a policy not only discourages slower and less advantaged students but also deprives abler ones of leadership training and democratic attitudes.

claims.text: The charge of cultural bias in tests and schooling is highly misleading or irrelevant because the root of unfairness is historical and economic, not cultural.
claims.source_passage: The reasons for the unfairness are not primarily cultural but historical and economic, making the charge of cultural bias in tests and schooling highly misleading, if not irrelevant.

claims.text: The principal unfairness in testing is the failure of the school system to prepare students for the competencies the tests measure.
claims.source_passage: The principal unfairness connected with testing consists in a failure to prepare students adequately for the competencies for which they are to be tested.

claims.text: The verbal section of the SAT is essentially a vocabulary test.
claims.source_passage: The verbal section of the SAT, for instance, is at bottom a vocabulary test.

claims.text: American public elementary schools provide neither equality of opportunity nor equality of result.
claims.source_passage: It is easily demonstrated that the public elementary schools of the United States provide neither equality of opportunity nor equality of result.

claims.text: The United States system affords significantly less equality of educational opportunity than most other developed nations.
claims.source_passage: Comparative studies show that our system affords far less equality of educational opportunity than exists in most developed nations.

claims.text: Fairness in testing is inseparable from fairness in the quality of schooling provided.
claims.source_passage: Fairness in testing cannot be separated from fairness in schooling.

claims.text: Focusing on equality of test results while ignoring underlying student incompetence and school ineffectiveness is a misguided approach.
claims.source_passage: Those who place emphasis on achieving equality of test results seem particularly misguided when they focus on tests and neglect the underlying realities denoted by the test results—incompetent students and ineffective schools.

claims.text: Attacking tests ('blaming the messenger') indicates a state of despair regarding the possibility of actually improving schools or student outcomes.
claims.source_passage: The tendency to blame the messenger instead of changing the news itself suggests a state of despair regarding the improvement of students and schools, and regarding substantial compensatory efforts to achieve equality of opportunity.

claims.text: The College Board's renorming of SAT scores based on minority participation is a form of sociological determinism that is condescending to minority students.
claims.source_passage: Such sociological determinism borders on condescension toward minority students and represents the same sort of mindset that antitesters display when they demand equality of test results whether or not underlying competencies are equalized.

claims.text: The LSAT is statistically accurate in predicting grades for both Black and White law students.
claims.source_passage: But the LSAT was statistically accurate in predicting the grades that both blacks and whites would make in law school.

claims.text: American public education has a differential effect on social classes, and consequently on ethnic and racial groups who belong disproportionately to disadvantaged classes.
claims.source_passage: American public education had then—and continues to have—a differential effect on social classes, and because of that more elemental and decisive fact, a differential effect on ethnic and racial groups who belong disproportionately to disadvantaged classes.

claims.text: Students from middle and upper classes become more competent in school because their home-provided 'intellectual capital' allows them to fill in the gaps of a mediocre, repetitive school system.
claims.source_passage: Students from middle and upper classes, coming from educated homes, learn more in school and become more competent than educationally less advantaged students because the intellectual capital derived from their homes enables them to derive a great deal more from gap-ridden and repetitive schooling than can students who are in no position to fill in the gaps with home-provided knowledge.

claims.text: In a mediocre school system, the competence gap between social classes expands as students progress through school.
claims.source_passage: In a mediocre school system, the competence gap between social classes widens during the school years.

claims.text: A coherent school system with definite year-by-year goals for all students makes systematic compensation possible and narrows the competence gap.
claims.source_passage: In a good, coherent school system with definite year-by-year goals for all students, early, systematic compensation becomes possible, and the competence gap is narrowed.

claims.text: Genuine equality of educational opportunity is impossible without effective, early compensatory measures that address the initial differential in intellectual capital.
claims.source_passage: Given the initial differential in intellectual capital between social classes, there can be no equality of educational opportunity without effective compensatory measures—the earlier the better.

claims.text: The claim that American 'diversity' and 'uniqueness' make it impossible to achieve educational equality is a much-exaggerated excuse to avoid learning from more successful nations.
claims.source_passage: Stating this much-exaggerated fact is perhaps meant to imply that it is impossible for us, because of our uniqueness, to achieve equality of educational opportunity, and to suggest that we have nothing to learn from nations that have better solved the problem of equal educational opportunity.

claims.text: Both the Taiwanese 'meritocratic' system and the Japanese 'egalitarian' system are fairer than the American system because they achieve higher competency and leave fewer students behind.
claims.source_passage: But clearly both Asian curves are fairer than the American curve, which leaves behind so many incompetent students, that it could not be called fair on either ideological principle.

claims.text: Fairness in testing is inextricably linked to fairness and excellence in schooling.
claims.source_passage: Fairness in testing cannot be separated from fairness in schooling, but as the graphs also suggest, fairness in schooling cannot be isolated from excellence in schooling.

claims.text: Educational principles that elicit high performance from advantaged students also elicit high performance from disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Fairness and excellence invariably go together in national systems of education because the educational principles and arrangements that elicit the best performances and highest competencies from advantaged students also elicit the best from disadvantaged students.

claims.text: Explicit, high achievement requirements for all students ensure that disadvantaged students gain essential intellectual capital not provided by their homes.
claims.source_passage: When there are very explicit requirements that all must meet, every student is assured of gaining from the school essential intellectual capital that was not derived from the home.

claims.text: The Jefferson-Mann vision of the democratic common school is currently being realized more effectively in Asia and Europe than in the United States.
claims.source_passage: It is a sad historical irony that, because of accidents of intellectual and institutional history, their ideal is being realized better in Asia and Europe than in the United States.

claims.text: Excellent national systems utilize arrangements where students perform demanding tasks, including instruction assistance, to stay challenged and avoid boredom.
claims.source_passage: In such systems, all children are brought up to the required high level, enabling the class as a whole to move forward step by step.

claims.text: High average achievement in national systems is explained by bringing all children to a required high level, allowing the class to move forward collectively.
claims.source_passage: This arrangement explains the high average level of achievement in excellent national systems, and it also explains how those systems achieve compensatory equalization of educational opportunity.

claims.text: The compensatory equalization of intellectual capital was a core element of the Jefferson-Mann vision of the democratic common school.
claims.source_passage: This compensatory equalization of intellectual capital was implicit in the Jefferson-Mann vision of the democratic common school.

claims.text: High-stakes tests serve functions—gatekeeping, monitoring, and incentives—that are essential to social fairness.
claims.source_passage: The final irony of the antitesting movement is that in the name of social fairness it opposes using high-stakes tests as gatekeepers, monitors, and incentives—functions that are essential to social fairness.

claims.text: No educational system has achieved or can achieve excellence and equity without effective monitoring and high incentives, including high-stakes testing.
claims.source_passage: Without effective monitoring and high incentives, including high-stakes testing programs, no educational system has achieved or could achieve excellence and equity.

claims.text: High-stakes tests are effective in motivating students to work hard.
claims.source_passage: John Bishop has shown in great detail the importance of high-stakes tests in motivating students to work hard.

claims.text: The Romantic idea that learning is natural and motivation is internal is an illusion.
claims.source_passage: The Romantic idea that learning is natural, and that the motivation for academic achievement comes from within, is an illusion

claims.text: The belief in natural learning is a barrier to social justice because disadvantaged students must be motivated to work harder than advantaged students.
claims.source_passage: The Romantic idea that learning is natural... is an illusion that forms one of the greatest barriers to social justice imaginable, since poor and disadvantaged students must be motivated to work even harder than advantaged students in order to achieve equality of educational opportunity.

claims.text: Educational achievement requires extrinsic motivation, discipline, toil, and sweat.
claims.source_passage: educational achievement comes [not] as naturally as leaves to a tree, [but] with extrinsic motivation, discipline, toil, or sweat.

claims.text: To overcome the illness of America's educational problems, we first need to abandon the cures currently being proposed.
claims.source_passage: To overcome the illness, we first need to abandon the cures.

claims.text: The fact that high-consequence tests act as spurs to effort supports an Augustinian view of human nature.
claims.source_passage: Tests that carry high consequences have been shown over and over again to act as spurs to effort—a fact that (to the distress of all Romantics) tends to support the Augustinian view of human nature.

claims.text: Using off-the-shelf tests as the primary tools for educational reform is an abuse that allows policymakers to avoid making difficult decisions about specific goals.
claims.source_passage: Second, it is an abuse of these off-the-shelf tests to rely on them as primary tools of educational reform—a convenient practice that enables policymakers to evade hard decisions about educational goals and strategies.

claims.text: Performance-based assessments often fail because simulations do not replicate the real world, and examinees behave differently in test settings than in real-life situations.
claims.source_passage: simulations are not the real world, and “examinees do not behave in the same way they would in real life”

claims.text: While it is desirable to encourage creativity in problem-solving, it is questionable whether such skills can actually be taught in a school setting.
claims.source_passage: It is certainly desirable to encourage creativity, but many believe it cannot be taught.

claims.text: The meritocratic test curve in Taiwan is bimodal, representing the statistical fusion of test results from a regular track and an elite track of schooling.
claims.source_passage: Taiwan’s curve is meritocratic... The meritocratic curve is bimodal, with two distinct bumps in the middle, representing a fusion of test results from the regular track and the elite track of schooling.

claims.text: Cultural identity is an accidental contingency of history rather than an essential, defining characteristic of a person's nature.
claims.source_passage: Cultural identity is a contingency—an accident, not an essence.

claims.text: The marketplace is the primary historical creator of great lingua francas, such as Koine and Latin, which are used to enable communication between strangers.
claims.source_passage: The marketplace is a commons. It erases ethnic distinctions. It has been the creator of the great lingua francas of the world—Koine (the language of the New Testament), Latin, and, now, English.

claims.text: In a good, coherent school system, early and systematic compensation narrows the competence gap between social classes.
claims.source_passage: In a good, coherent school system with definite year-by-year goals for all students, early, systematic compensation becomes possible, and the competence gap is narrowed.

evidence.description: George Madaus's summary of 36 printed sources (1990–1992) documenting the attack on standardized tests and the claims made for 'authentic' assessment.
evidence.source_reference: Madaus, 'A Technological and Historical Consideration of Equity Issues...'
evidence.source_passage: George Madaus... has excellently summarized the current scene in the following assertions, which he took from thirty-six printed sources published between 1990 and 1992

evidence.description: Historical pattern of Romantic progressivism dating back to the 1920s that rejects tests as part of a 'factory model.'
evidence.source_reference: Not specified in this section beyond general history
evidence.source_passage: The campaign against giving students tests is an integral part of a Romantic progressivism that goes back to the 1920s.

evidence.description: Madaus's collection of thirty-six distinct attacks on standardized tests from the early 1990s.
evidence.source_passage: In the 1970s... Madaus collected his thirty-six attacks on standardized tests.

evidence.description: Analysis of the SAT score decline and the demographics of declining scores in Iowa.
evidence.source_reference: Jencks, 'What’s Behind the Drop in Test Scores?' (1978)
evidence.source_passage: Jencks also refuted the NEA claim that standardized tests were “biased in favor of white middle-class culture” by pointing out that scores had declined sharply in Iowa

evidence.description: The historical implementation of a test-free and grade-free environment at the Teachers College demonstration school.
evidence.source_passage: When William Heard Kilpatrick designed his demonstration school at Teachers College... one of his first innovations... was to abolish tests and grades.

evidence.description: Historical account of William Heard Kilpatrick's demonstration school at Teachers College where he abolished tests and grades.
